_comment: Path patterns Defender exclusions must NEVER cover without explicit user override. Broad exclusions here defeat the point of Defender for the machine's most-attacked directories. ps/apply/defender.ps1 refuses any exclusion that matches these patterns unless -IKnowWhatImDoing is set. Match rules: paths are compared case-insensitively; a plan path P is refused if any pattern here is a case-insensitive substring or exact match of P AFTER env-var expansion. Added 2026-07-07 after module audit.

refuseUnlessOverride.why: Excluding the entire OS drive is never appropriate for path-based exclusion.
Excluding all users' profiles removes Defender from Downloads, Desktop, Documents, temp folders — the exact places malware lands.
Public folder is a common malware drop target.
Same problem as C:\Users — the current user's whole profile including Downloads and Desktop.
Downloads folder is the #1 initial landing spot for browser-delivered malware.
Desktop is commonly used to store just-downloaded files.
Documents is where phishing attachments (docx macros, pdf) typically land.
TEMP is where installers unpack and where in-memory-then-disk droppers write. Excluding it hides the whole decompression phase.
AppData\Roaming is the persistence directory for user-mode malware (Startup shortcuts, Chrome extensions, PowerShell profiles).
LocalAppData is where portable apps + Chromium extensions + persistence artifacts live.
Excluding Windows itself hides system-file impersonation and drivers.
ProgramData is another common persistence location.

warnButAllow.why: Large + noisy but has occasionally hosted supply-chain malware. Allow but warn.
Git internals. Allow but warn — cloned repos can carry malicious hook scripts.
